10X Advisor Network: Welcome back to our 10X Business Buying Case study series.
Today we’re talking with Oliver Fernandez. Oliver is a successful entrepreneur who’s
leveraged certain construction contracts into a $40mm empire. He’s been working
with Brandon Dawson and Grant Cardone through Cardone Ventures and other
programs… and recently acquired a flooring business with their help. Today we’re
here to talk with him about it so you can learn the details of a real deal. This deal has
an interesting origin story, so we’re excited to get into it. Oliver, thanks very much for
joining us.
Oliver Fernandez: It’s great to be here.
10X Advisor Network: Let’s start with your background. Who are you… what’s your
career… and how did you find yourself acquiring a business?
Oliver Fernandez: Sure. I own a construction business that's headquartered here in
Washington, DC. And when I first moved to DC, I started buying and renovating
properties, buying a single family home and converting it into a renovated property
that people could live in and rent out from me.
And while I was down here, I started to see all of the federal buildings that were
everywhere here in DC. And I realized that there were actual contractors that were
contracted to go in there and make the repairs and to make the updates to these
buildings. And I was curious about how I could be one of those contractors. How could
I be on that short list of contractors being able to go into those buildings?
And I made a couple contacts and those contacts helped me develop the understanding
of how to go and market to the government. Once I started marketing to the
government, I obviously fell on my face a lot in the beginning. I didn't really
understand what to say and I didn't understand what they were even looking for.
As I continued to work on my craft and continue to get better, I started to be able to
position the company to be in a situation where they would actually want to work with
us. So I remember getting my first contract with The Fish and Wildlife Service and it
was down in Sanibel Island in Florida. It wasn't even here in Washington, DC.
I wasn't good enough to get a contract here, but I got a project down in Sanibel Island
and, man, this was a $500,000 job. At the time, my business wasn't doing well. So we
had about $500,000 in debt and this job was my lifeline as I was transitioning the
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 20 --

business into doing work directly for the owner instead of being a subcontractor. And
we finished that project.
And then all of a sudden, when I was making my calls to my customers, I was calling
with conviction because now I had a reference, a past performance that was mine. It
wasn't just my partners anymore. It was my past performance. So man, I hit those
phones so hard and next thing you know I had 10 jobs all spread out in Albany,
Georgia, places you probably never even dreamed of going because I never dreamed of
going there. I was in Byron, Georgia, Tifton, Georgia. I was in Panama City, Florida,
Parris Island, South Carolina.
Then things just blew up from there. And we had to bring on people, we had to bring
on team members because obviously I couldn't be in all those different locations at
one time.
So it was amazing. And now we've completed over $70 million worth of contracts.
We've actually partnered with Grant Cardone and we're now starting to buy
businesses together. And we just finished our first year with the first business that we
bought, man.
It was amazing. And it was amazing because we built such a great team so quickly to
go and be able to complete this transaction, be able to keep the business moving
forward in its first full year of operation under our operations and grow the business
from a $5 million business to an $8 million business in the first year.
10X Advisor Network: That's great! Can you give us some background on the
business you purchased and getting started with the acquisition process?
Oliver Fernandez:. Prior to going to Grant Cardone's sales boot camp and meeting
Brandon Dawson there, I never thought about buying a business. And the reason why I
never thought about buying a business is because I was so deep in my own business
that I couldn't even see any other opportunities.
We were making a lot of money, but I was just so ingrained in the day to day activities.
I used to pride myself on working 20 hour-days and never taking a vacation, or we'd
take vacations, but it would be the quick weekend vacations or the work away
vacations where we'd work while we were away and I really couldn't leave my phone
and the computer back in the hotel room.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 20 --

So I never thought about buying a business because I was so overwhelmed with what I
currently had on my plate. And it was a lot. We were doing around $10 million a year
and you know how construction is, I mean, there are a lot of moving pieces, there's a
lot of coordination, there's a lot of materials that need to be bought, there's a lot of
coordinating with installers and contractors and subcontractors.
So we had our plate full. So I never considered buying a business until I started
learning the methodologies that Grant Cardone and Brandon Dawson teach. And that
is how to build a team, scale a team and to get them more excited about the work than
you are.
Because a lot of times, us as business owners, we understand that the work doesn't
end at 5:00. We understand that the work ends when the work is done. So we're willing
to put in all the extra effort, but then we're around a bunch of people that want to get
out at 5:00. And sometimes that is the case, but sometimes we have to stay additional
time.
And working with Grant Cardone and Brandon Dawson, I understood how to inspire
my team and build a team that wanted to work those extra hours to get the results and
not only for themselves and for the business, but to get the results for themselves as
well.
10X Advisor Network: How did you find the business you acquired?
Oliver Fernandez: So my story with finding the business was actually really
interesting. And it was another thing that I learned from Brandon Dawson. He always
talks about this word “sprinkling.” Sprinkling is telling people about what you're
doing and where you're going and how you're going to impact the world.
And that's honestly how he got me interested. I was interested in what he was doing
because I wanted to be a part of where he was going. And I took that idea of sprinkling
and started telling the people that followed me and the people that cared about me
and where I was going.
I was telling my friends from back home that were working in construction “Hey, if
you ever find an opportunity that it's too big for you guys, please reach out. I have some
resources that we could go after together.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 20 --

And one conversation with one of my great friends back home, we've been friends
since we were five years old, brought this amazing opportunity to us.
He was working for a guy who had owned a business for 30 years. He was 60 plus years
old. I don't know the exact age, but he wanted to get out of the business. He didn't
want to do it anymore. And my friend was just an employee.
My friend was making good money. It was plus six figures, but he didn't have the
capital set aside to be able to go in and purchase the business and he didn't
understand how to be a business owner. So he brought the opportunity to me and he
said, "Hey, Oliver, my owner wants to sell. What are your thoughts on this?"
Obviously I was like, "Hey, we're definitely interested.”
I like to commit first and then figure the rest out later. So we definitely were
interested. We were excited about the opportunity.
We wanted to see some financials. So we got the owner interested in us because we
said we were interested, then he sent us over financials and then we could start
digging through the numbers.
And in the beginning, the numbers looked horrible. A lot of business owners, they use
their business as their personal piggy bank. And the business based off of the numbers
that we saw wasn't even profitable.
But then we started going through and doing a bunch of add-backs for taking out all
the owner expenses and stuff and then we saw how profitable this business really was
and what the opportunity really was.
We used the team at Cardone Ventures to help us analyze this business as well,
because they're surgeons with this stuff.
They understand how to go in and what to add-back and what not to add-back. So we
went in and we partnered with them to go and surgically go through these financial
statements to see what the real opportunity was.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 20 --

And when we really saw the opportunity, we're like, wow, we're buying this thing on a
great basis and we have a lot of opportunity to grow here.
10X Advisor Network: What were the steps, from the introduction to taking over the
business, in your acquisition process?
Oliver Fernandez: Yeah. So when we first looked at this business, it was really a non-
traditional acquisition process. And what I mean by non-traditional is, we were
working directly with the owner from day one.
A lot of times these businesses are listed on a public website, you need to sign a
nondisclosure agreement to even get information about the business. Whereas, in our
case, we were working directly with the business owner.
And the reason why we were working directly with the business owner is because my
friend that was working for this business owner brought us the deal. When you're
working with the business owner, the one thing you really need to really understand
the business is you need the financial statements.
You need both the profit and loss statement, you need the balance sheet, and you need
the cash flow statement. And I promise you, if you're working with the business owner
directly, these financial statements, the profit and loss statement, the balance sheet,
and the cash flow statement are going to be a mess.
One of the benefits of working with a broker instead of going directly to the business
owner is that the broker will help massage these financial statements so that they are
actually digestible for us. And what I mean for us is for an investor. So working
directly with the business owner, we got the profit and loss, we got the balance sheet,
and we got the cash flow statements.
They were a complete mess. So we had to go in there and we had to go and take away
some of the business owner's expenses that he ran through the business. That’s what
they call adding back. You add back so that you can get a seller discretionary earnings.
The Seller's Discretionary Earnings is the cash flow of the business that you would be
able to receive as the new owner of the business. And then what typically happens with
these businesses is that they value on off a multiple off of that seller discretionary
earnings.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 20 --

So when we did our math, we saw that the business had $600,000 in seller
discretionary earnings. And then the business owner wanted $600,000 for the
business. So that was a 1X multiple. Now, if the business owner wanted $1.2 million
and the seller discretionary earnings were only $600,000, then that would've been a
2X multiple.
So out of 1X multiple, $600,000 in earnings and the business owner wanted $600,000,
we thought we were getting a great deal. And after a full year of ownership and a full
year of filing taxes the business was a grand slam and I'm super excited that we
actually went through with the transaction.
10X Advisor Network: Who did you use as your deal team?
Oliver Fernandez: The Cardone Ventures team provided 90% of everything that we
needed in house. There were a few things that we needed to go and outsource, and that
was attorneys to put together the operating agreements. And honestly, you want to
use attorneys for all of that stuff.
You don't want to be trying to recreate that stuff in house and you personally don't
want to be doing it, especially if you're bringing on partners and you're really trying to
do the thing the right way. But no, this is definitely a team game. And that's the cool
thing about buying a $5 million business.
We’re looking to grow this $5 million business to $100 million. And we're doing this
as a team. It's not just one person going in and trying to recreate this whole thing. The
opportunity is big enough to bring a team in so that we have all of the areas of the
business covered.
And honestly, not having to do everything myself after buying this business has been
a huge benefit for me. I mean, there have been so many years where I had to do
everything.
And one of the reasons why I had to do everything is because I started this business.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 20 --

When you buy a business, the business is already moving forward. It already has
customers. It already has a team there. There are already floor installers. There's a
showroom. There's people working the showroom. There's a warehouse. There's
people working the warehouse.
We’re just going in and we're tweaking the mindset of the people that are there, but
we're not having to go in and recreate everything.
So we're going in and we're basically buying a team and we need to just train that
team on our new mindset, which does take some time, but it's a lot easier than trying
to recreate everything by yourself and alone.
10X Advisor Network: What were there things that the seller needed to see from you
to make them comfortable selling to you?
Oliver Fernandez: When we first got in contact with the buyer, we already had a
really close relationship with him because the employee of the seller was my really
good friend.
So what I wanted to do is I wanted to sit down with the seller and I wanted him to
speak because a lot of times with selling a business, people are selling their baby.
They've been working days and nights for 30 years with this thing. So they care a lot
about it. And I literally sat down with him and I asked him one question “tell me how
you started this?” And I mean, the entire night went by. And we spent three or four
hours and he just talked and talked and talked.
After he was done talking, we were getting up from the table, I said, “What do you think
of us as buyers?” He's like, “Man, I love it.”
And I just sat there and laughed because we didn't say one word. We just let him talk.
He felt so good about the situation because he was telling us his history.
That's a unique situation because oftentimes we're now looking at other businesses to
buy, we're working with brokers, and we're working with these brokers and we're
going to the locations of these businesses and we're spending an hour there.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 20 --

It's typically after hours when the rest of the team is gone, because a lot of these
business owners, they don't want their employees or their team members or the
subcontractors knowing that they're selling.
So we'll go there after hours and we'll spend an hour there, but it's usually kind of
quick.
Whereas that first conversation with the owner that we purchased it from, it was four
hours long. I mean, he told us every story about the business. So that was a little
unique.
Typical transactions don't always go that way. A typical transaction will go with you
meeting with a broker and then that broker kind of working that transaction or
working that relationship.
And you probably spend an hour at the facility. And then obviously you get your
financials, you get your profit and loss statement, your balance sheet, and your cash
flow statements and then you can underwrite the deal from there and see if it's a good
fit for you and your team.
10X Advisor Network: Did your friend who was an employee and brought the deal to
you have a deal structure in mind, or he just saw an opportunity without really
knowing the specifics of how it would work out?
Oliver Fernandez: Yeah. The friend that brought the deal to me didn't understand
how to structure a deal. I mean, he knew that he was bringing the opportunity to us
and he knew that he wanted some ownership of it. But he didn't really understand how
to structure it. And honestly, I didn't really understand how to structure it.
That was one of the superpowers of partnering with Brandon Dawson and the Cardone
Ventures team. They understand how to structure deals because honestly, I was
bringing a lot of capital to the table, I was bringing a lot of experience. And what I
mean by experiences, and Brandon will often talk about this, is he talks about the law
of the lid.
I was the highest lid of the new potential owners of this business. I was the one that
had owned a business before. None of these other people had owned a business before.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 20 --

They were still great people, hardworking, all of those things, but they just didn't have
that understanding that I had. They didn't have the confidence that I had.
And it's rightfully so. They didn't have the 10 years of experience of owning and
running a business and going through the trials and tribulations of running a business
than I had. So their lids were not as high as mine.
On my own, I risked setting up the acquisition where I potentially was the highest lid…
bringing the most money to the deal… AND I would have potentially been voted out of
this business in the future, even though I was the person who handled the purchase.
Luckily, I partnered with Brandon Dawson and the Cardone Ventures team to
structure it properly so that the business could continue to move forward and I could
stay in my position of leadership and not have to be pushed out for some reason like if
my other partners wanted to go in a different direction.
10X Advisor Network: How did you end up partnering with your friend in the
business?
Oliver Fernandez: We knew my friend was going to be the operating partner because
I already had my own construction business and I didn't have the time to spend every
waking moment there to figure out all the problems. And there's going to be problems
like any business.
Any business is going to have problems. So you need to have someone boots on the
ground to be the operating partner. So my friend was going to be the operating partner
there on site and that was definitely understood.
Then we had another partner that was our director of finance, and he was really good
with numbers. And we were able to take him from a billion dollar company. And the
only way you get people to leave a billion dollar company is you have to paint a really
big picture for them. And we painted a really big picture:
I told him: “We're looking to buy this flooring business and we're looking to grow and scale
this thing to $100 million in the next five years. And then we want this thing to be a billion
dollar company. And for it could be a billion dollar company, we first need to hit this $100
million. So let's just set that goal out there right here right now and let's put the energy and
effort behind this business to go hit that.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 20 --

And he ended up leaving that company and joining us. His compensation package
required him to invest in the deal, and in return he also was going to get some equity
in the deal. He had never been a business owner prior to this transaction either.
So I was excited to provide and help provide this opportunity for people that I cared
about, people that were hardworking and people that hadn't really tasted the honey of
owning a business.
And, man, they got a taste of it this year and they're more excited than ever, man.
So let me break down the deal even a little bit further…
We had myself, we had Grant Cardone, Brandon Dawson, and Cardone Ventures, we
had my buddy, Clayton, who was going to be the operating officer, and then we had
Brian, who was going to be the finance director.
The way we worked that out was we knew we had to bring experience to the deal and
then we also needed to bring capital to the deal.
So I used some previous understandings and then worked with Brandon on how we
were going to split that up.
We came to the understanding after working it through with Brandon Dawson and
Cardone Ventures that we wanted to do 50-50.
And what I mean by 50-50 would mean the operations side of the business would get
50% of the deal and then the capital, the money being brought to the deal would get
50% of the deal.
Then, when you break down the operations side, there are five different facets.
We have our sales… we have our marketing… we have our human resources… we have
finance… and we also have our day to day operations like project coordination.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 20 --

Once we broke down all that stuff, we came to the understanding that my operating
partner was going to be doing a majority of that work. We decided that that was going
to be around 30%. That left 20% for Cardone Ventures, 20% for myself, and 20% for
the finance department. And then the operating partner wasn't able to bring any
capital to the table except for, I think he brought in $50,000.
So he got a smaller piece of the financial 50% and he got a larger piece of the
operation 50%, and rightfully so. I mean, the kid worked so many hours. I mean, it
was crazy that first year getting everything off the ground and being able to put the
right butts in the right seats.
He definitely earned every little bit of that operational equity. So then we had the
financial side 50%, and we had the operation side 50%. We broke apart each of that
accordingly and then we continued to move forward with the deal.
10X Advisor Network: Why did this business trade for a 1X multiple? And how did
you finance the purchase?
Oliver Fernandez: Small businesses typically trade anywhere between a 1X and 3X --
sometimes up to 5X -- multiple. And for our situation, we ended up getting a 1X
multiple. The reason why I believe we got a 1X multiple was because the partner that
we were buying this with was an employee of the business.
And the previous owner didn't really understand how to create standard operating
procedures, they didn't understand how to build a team. So basically everything was
running through our partner that we were bringing on to this business.
So if, for some reason, the business owner didn't sell it to us, the key employee that
was my friend would've left and the business would have crumbled. Because there was
nobody there that had intimate knowledge of all the moving pieces in the business.
So we ended up getting it at a 1X multiple, and this was a number that the business
owner was happy with. And at the same time, this was a guy that didn't think he was
going to get anything for his business. He was just going to close it down. He had no
family. What I mean by no family is he had no kids and he really didn't know anybody
who was interested in buying a flooring business.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 20 --

So now he was getting $600,000 when he thought he was going to just have to shut it
down. And he used that $600,000 to retire. Most people in the flooring business don't
ever retire. And if they do retire, they don't get $600,000 to go retire. And then he still
owns a bunch of real estate. He actually owns the showroom that we're at.
We didn't buy the real estate with the transaction. So we're still renting from him. So
we created a win-win scenario.
We took a business and a lot of responsibility off his back and we gave him a big chunk
of money and then we still gave him cash flow from the business in the term of rents
from the real estate.
10X Advisor Network: How did you look at the assets like that real estate when you
were in the negotiating stage?
Oliver Fernandez: Yeah. So when we were working through this with the business
owner, obviously he had the business portion and then he also had real estate.
There were three warehouses, each valued at $200,000 a piece. So that was $600,000.
And then he wanted another $600,000 for the showroom, but the showroom, he had
owned in a partnership with a neighboring business, and the neighboring business
was some sort of a pawn shop or something.
So he thought the total value of the showroom real estate was $1.2 million.
So $1.2 million plus the $600,000 for the warehouses was $1.8 million, and then
another $600,000 for the business would've brought it to $2.4 million.
We knew the real estate was valuable, but we also knew that we were going to have to
come up with a lot more cash to buy the real estate.
And we knew we'd be able to purchase this real estate at a later date and we also knew
that the business owner would be more likely to get rid of the headaches, which was
the business versus getting rid of all the real estate as well.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 20 --

So we used the real estate to almost create this win-win scenario with the business
owner, whereas he keeps the real estate, which is a big asset for him, he also keeps the
cash flow of that real estate and then we buy the business.
One of the reasons why I love business is they can cash flow way better than any piece
of real estate.
We already knew what the rents were on the real estate.
The rent was $5,000 a month.
Even if you doubled the rent, it's still only $10,000.
Whereas this business, we took it from $5 million to $8 million and we made a million
in profit. And we could always land a $250,000 job tomorrow. We could always land a
million dollar job tomorrow.
With the real estate, it's kind of fixed. There's a lot of value in real estate and I love
investing my money in real estate. I have over $5 million invested in real estate, but
for this specific play of buying a business, it wasn't going to benefit us in this current
scenario.
We wanted to put as little money down, but be able to get an asset that could cash flow
tremendously for us.
10X Advisor Network: Was your financing strategy the vision from the get go? Or did
your approach evolve?
Oliver Fernandez: That's a really good question. When we were going to purchase
this business, we had a number of options. We had owner financing, and that was one
of the reasons why my partner originally brought me into the scenario because the
previous owner said that he would owner-finance the deal.
Once we got to the table and started actually walking through the deal, he pulled that
back really quickly. He didn't want to owner-finance the deal. And the reason why he
didn't want to owner-finance the deal is because he understood how businesses can
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 20 --

be. Businesses can be up or it could be down. And it's almost like holding sand in your
hand.
It could slip away from you really quickly and you lose all the value of it. So he pulled
the rug out from under us and said, no more owner-financing, which was fine.
And then our second option was to go to a bank. We went to the bank, but they looked
at his financials and they thought the business doesn't make any money. They weren’t
willing to loan us any money on this business.
So we had to back up a little bit and say, how are we going to buy this business?
And because I had previously been running a business for 10 years, we had some
money stock piled away for investment.
That's one of the biggest things that I took away from partnering with Grant Cardone
and Cardone Ventures and Brandon Dawson is that once you stop working in the
business and start working on the business, you can start to see the opportunities
around the business.
So I was able to use some of the dry powder that we had in the business and start to
make strategic moves for the business. So we were able to raise the full amount of the
money that needed to be used to purchase the business, and we raised that through
investors and I was one of the investors.
My company was one of the investors in the deal, Cardone Ventures was one of the
investors in the deal.
In fact, all the partners were one of the investors in the deal. That's one of my
principles, is that if you want distributions from the business, then you need to make
a contribution to the business.
And then the reason why I like that is because it prevents people who are putting a lot
of work into the business from coming to you later on and saying, hey, I want some
ownership equity in the business.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 20 --

I'm totally good with giving you ownership equity in the business, you just need to
make a contribution to the business in terms of taking money out of your pocket and
putting it into the business and then you can get distribution from the business.
Now, there are times where we'll do profit interest and stuff like that, but if you want
pure equity of the business, then you need to make a contribution.
If you want profits from the business, I'm totally good with sharing that for team
members that are working hard and giving them bonuses out of the profits of the
business, but equity of the business takes a contribution.
So we had owner-finance that was on the table that was pulled out from under us
because the business owners understood that business can be very volatile, it could be
greater or it could be bad and it could slip right from underneath you pretty quickly.
The banks wouldn't lend to us because the previous owner was running so many of his
expenses in the business that the bank was like, this business is not profitable. They
didn’t understand add-backs.
So the only other option we had was to raise the money from investors, and we were
able to do that, and we provided tremendous returns for our investors. It was basically
over 100% in year one for the returns.
10X Advisor Network: So from your friend contacting you to taking the keys, how
long was the deal process for you guys?
Oliver Fernandez: The initial contact with my friend started probably a year before
we purchased the business from the owner. And the reason why it took a year is
because a lot of these businesses are like the owner's baby. He had owned it for 30
years. There's a lot of emotional connections to it.
They oftentimes will say the strongest thing in the world is someone's routine. And
this guy had a routine that had been ingrained for 30 years, showing up at the
showroom, sitting at his desk, working behind his computer. For 30 years, this guy
had done this.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 20 --

So we had to work through those psychological changes that he was going to have to
make by selling this business to us. And that process took a year. And one of the
catalysts for him to say, hey, enough is enough, I'm done was COVID.
A week after COVID, he reached out to my now-partner who was his employee and
said, hey, I'm ready to sell, let's make this thing happen, January 2021.
And it was less than a year from when COVID was announced. And we made the
transaction happen. We made the transaction happen even after he took away the
owner financing, even after the banks wouldn't lend to us. We raised the money from
investors.
And honestly, I think that was also one of the reasons why we got a 1X multiple,
because he knew that he was really constricted to selling to us because if he brought
this deal to the street and let other investors look at it, they weren't going to see the
profits that were in the business.
The only reason we knew about the profits in the business is because my partner was
an employee in the business and he knew what were real expenses of the business and
what were expenses of the previous owner.
10X Advisor Network: When you took the keys, how did that first day as a new
owner of this business look?
Oliver Fernandez: Yeah. When we took over the business, I mean, obviously we were
all smiles. I mean, here is something that we've been talking about for a year. And now
all of a sudden, we own it. We're in the driver's seat now. So we were all smiles. We
were very excited to have the business. And one of the first things that we did that day
was we got the entire team together.
And there's 25 people that worked at the business and we got them all together and we
met at the warehouse that morning and we casted the vision for what we were
planning to do with the organization.
It wasn't this multi-hour meeting, it was a quick 15 minute meeting, but at least
everybody understood there was new ownership, they understood where we were
going, and they understood that they were going to be a part of it.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 20 --

And the reason why they knew that they were going to be a part of it is because we
included that in that meeting. We let them know that the same opportunity that
Clayton, the previous employee now part-owner, had in this organization, every team
member here would have, and they would have to still step up and earn that
opportunity, and we were going to do big things together.
And so that meeting was great. And then obviously, we're starting the business,
everything is moving forward. In January, February, March., things were going great
because we already had customers. That's one of the benefits of buying these existing
businesses is because you do have customers. We already had customers coming in.
We already had team members in place to service those customers.
But as we continued to dig into what we had, we realized that things seemed great on
the surface, but we were starting to see some problems that were starting to arise. And
some of those problems were the profit margins of the previous owner's business
structure.
And we were using the previous owners' business structure in the beginning because
oftentimes when you go into a business, you don't want to change everything in the
beginning. You want to at least give it six months to monitor things, but you don't
want to change everything in the beginning.
And the reason why you don't want to change everything in the beginning is you don't
need a bunch of team members leaving. Imagine if we bought a business with 25 team
members and then all of a sudden 25 team members just walked up the door because
we changed a bunch of processes. So we were working with the existing team to tweak
these issues that we were finding.
So we implemented a margin process instead of just going with a $2 or specific dollar
amount increase on the product and on the services. So those are some of the nitty
gritty things that we worked through to make sure our margin was correct because
there was a point where it got a little scary.
There were things on the income statement that were very opaque. It wasn't clear to
us on how it was going to play out. Then, all of a sudden, once we're now behind the
driver's seat, these things are really playing out for us. And we had to make some
changes after that six-month time period to really get the profits of the business
accelerating the way we thought they were going to accelerate.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 20 --

10X Advisor Network: How do you interact with the business now as an owner?
Oliver Fernandez: The way I operate in this business is I work with my partner as the
brain, right? And what I mean by the brain is I understand through working with
Grant Cardone and Brandon Dawson and Cardone Ventures, how to find the customers
that you want in your business.
And once you understand the customers that are your best customers, the ones that
pay you the most, the ones that you make the most money with, the ones that you like
working with, once you understand that, then you can focus on them and grow them.
And now you turn five into 10 and 10 into 20 and 20 into 50, and you get more of those
customers.
So what I did is my partner, that's the operating partner, they handle all the day to
day, but we'll sit down through Zoom calls because they're in New Hampshire and I'm
in Washington, DC, or just in a regular phone call, and we'll sit down twice a week,
once on Tuesday and we'll walk through all the financials of the business.
We walk through estimates that have gone out, the deals that have closed, we know
what our close percentage is, we know where our revenue is, we know what our profit
margins are. And that's why we were able to make the changes that we were able to
make is because we're monitoring all of this stuff once a week. So we have that call.
And then the second call is how we're going to dig into and expand our customer base.
And a part of that call is also how are we going to dial in operations? Because the
previous owner was working off of a whiteboard and they literally had Monday,
Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday on the whiteboard.
Now, with my prior construction business experience and with Cardone Ventures'
experience, I understood that for us to really have something that was going to be
valuable, we all needed to be working off of the same sheet of music. And when we're
all working off the same sheet of music, we can play a tune that is actually enjoyable.
So we took everybody's little spreadsheets and everybody's little whiteboards, and we
combined it into one spreadsheet so everybody could work off the same sheet of
music. And I big picture helped bring that into process and then obviously, the onsite
team was the one that put the information in there on a day to day basis and make it a
reality.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 20 --

10X Advisor Network: What would you do differently on this acquisition if you could
go back?
Oliver Fernandez: If I had to go back, what I would do differently is I would keep a
closer eye on the operations. And what I mean by the operations is we had brought in
somebody that was going to help be a project coordinator. And they were going to
coordinate between our subcontractors, our in-house installers, material being
delivered and the homeowners.
There was a lot of coordination that needed to be done. If that coordination isn't
moving at a really fast pace, the business can't produce. We can get our sales, but we
can't produce. So we were looking at our sales numbers and we were having million
dollar, million and a half dollar months. But this isn't selling a digital product where
once the sale is done, you just deliver the product.
We have to go out there and install. So our sales numbers were high, but our
installation was low. We weren't installing what we needed to install per month to
actually earn that revenue. So I would keep a closer eye on what we were actually
installing, especially if you're a service based business.
Your sales numbers need to be high, but your installations also need to be high as well.
So in the beginning of the year, our installation number was $300,000, $400,000,
$500,000. And then that's where we were getting crushed with our profit margins
because our profit margins were good if we would maintain above a $500,000 a month
of installation.
But if we didn't do that, then we were getting squeezed on our net profit. So as we
continued throughout the year and we started really focusing on that, we got it up to
installing a million dollars a month. And then that's when we were able to create
tremendous amounts of profit.
So really, we did horrible the first six months of the year and the last six months of the
year is where we made our profit. And the reason why we made our profit is because
we dialed in and started looking at the right number, and the right number wasn't just
the sales number, it wasn't just the revenue number. It was the installation number.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 19 of 20 --

And once we understood the right number to look at... And we understood the right
number after working with Cardone Ventures and breaking down all of the different
revenue streams, and we realized that we were looking at the wrong thing in the
beginning. And once we started looking at the right thing, we started growing and
standing, and we started hitting and creating massive profit for the business.
10X Advisor Network: Thanks so much! This was super valuable. And it shed light
on a lot of different approaches to acquisitions than our previous conversations.
